coredata.dc:title: Citric acid-assisted in situ preparation of MoIn2S4/CQDs with few-layer promotes charge transfer and enhances photocatalytic activity

coredata.dc:description: Ternary metal chalcogenides as promising photocatalysts and introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a dopant to enhance their photocatalytic activity. In this study, MoIn2S4 and MoIn2S4/CQDs composite photocatalysts were successfully synthesized utilizing an in-situ hydrothermal method, in which citric acid served as both the carbon source and surfactant. Among the prepared catalysts, the MoIn2S4/CQDs composite photocatalyst with the optimal ratio (MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g) exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, achieving a removal rate of 94.4 % for methylene blue (MB) after 60 min of simulated solar illumination. The superior performance was primarily attributed to the upconversion photoluminescence effect of CQDs, which enhanced the light absorption of MoIn2S4. Also, the few-layer configuration of MoIn2S4 as a matrix possessed numerous active sites to significantly minimize the aggregation of CQDs. Furthermore, the CQDs acted as electron acceptors and transport centers, inhibiting the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and accelerating electron transfer. Additionally, through trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests, the primary active species responsible for photocatalytic degradation was identified as superoxide radicals (·O2 –). Based on these findings, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g was proposed. This study provided a novel, stable and efficient MoIn2S4 modified with CQDs photocatalyst for water treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ternary metal chalcogenides as promising photocatalysts and introducing carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as a dopant to enhance their photocatalytic activity. In this study, MoIn2S4 and MoIn2S4/CQDs composite photocatalysts were successfully synthesized utilizing an in-situ hydrothermal method, in which citric acid served as both the carbon source and surfactant. Among the prepared catalysts, the MoIn2S4/CQDs composite photocatalyst with the optimal ratio (MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g) exhibited the highest photocatalytic performance, achieving a removal rate of 94.4 % for methylene blue (MB) after 60 min of simulated solar illumination. The superior performance was primarily attributed to the upconversion photoluminescence effect of CQDs, which enhanced the light absorption of MoIn2S4. Also, the few-layer configuration of MoIn2S4 as a matrix possessed numerous active sites to significantly minimize the aggregation of CQDs. Furthermore, the CQDs acted as electron acceptors and transport centers, inhibiting the rapid recombination of photogenerated carriers and accelerating electron transfer. Additionally, through trapping experiments and electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) tests, the primary active species responsible for photocatalytic degradation was identified as superoxide radicals (·O2 –). Based on these findings, a possible photocatalytic mechanism for MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g was proposed. This study provided a novel, stable and efficient MoIn2S4 modified with CQDs photocatalyst for water treatment.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In previous studies, semiconductor photocatalytic technology has been a popular choice among researchers due to its unique advantages in pollutant degradation, which are environmentally friendly and sustainable [1]. Therefore, the quest for a facilely prepared photocatalyst with high degradation efficiency and excellent cycling stability becomes paramount. In recent years, binary chalcogenide compounds A2B3 (A = Al, Ga, In; B = S, Se, Te) have emerged as a focal point of research owing to their outstanding photoelectric, unique electronic structures, and tunable band gaps, holding vast promise in the field of optoelectronics [2,3]. It has been reported that through the synergistic effect of multiple elements more photovoltaic semiconductor with appropriate bandgap and superior performance can be designed by introducing the third metal element into A2B3 [4]. Thus, the novel ternary metal chalcogenides XA2B4 (X = Zn, Ca, Cd; A = Al, Ga, In; B = S, Se, Te) have garnered increasing attention [5]. For instance, Chenbin Liu et al. fabricated a heterostructure comprising a single layer of ZnIn2S4 rich in S vacancies and MoS2 QDs with excellent photocatalytic hydrogen reaction (PHR) activity [6]. Chao Liu et al. synthesized a Ti3C2 MXene/CaIn2S4 Schottky heterojunction photocatalyst possessed outstanding photostability [7]. However, despite the extensive research on XA2B4 ternary phase materials in the field of photocatalytic degradation, MoIn2S4 has yet to be reported in the field of photocatalytic degradation.

The novel ternary compound MoIn2S4 thin film was first prepared by Gentian Yue et al. in 2020 and applied in dye-sensitized solar cells as an electrode material [8]. However, due to the typically narrow band gap of ternary metal sulfides, electrons and holes are prone to recombination, which limits the catalytic performance of pure-phase MoIn2S4 [9–11]. Therefore, strategies such as morphology regulation [12], heterostructure construction [13], surfactant modified [14], and carbon material modification [15] are essential to modify MoIn2S4 to enhance its photocatalytic performance. Among them, surfactants can regulate the morphology of the products to obtain a larger specific surface area to provide more catalytic active sites to improve the catalytic performance [16]. Guidong Yang et al. reported the in-situ growth of abundant and well dispersed ZnIn2S4 nanosheets on N-La2Ti2O with a larger specific surface area through surfactant (trisodium citrate) for morphology regulation [17]. Carbon materials have excellent electron transfer ability, which can promote charge separation to compensate for the defect of easy recombination of electron holes in MoIn2S4 [18–20] .

As zero-dimensional (0D) amorphous and highly disordered carbon nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are gaining popularity as photocatalytic modified materials due to their advantages, including easy preparation, low cost, non-toxicity, and strong optical absorption [21,22]. Xinjuan Liu et al. successfully synthesized three-dimensional flower-shaped microspheres of ZnIn2S4@CQDs using fructose (C6H12O6) as a carbon source through a simple one-step solvothermal method [23]. Moreover, CQDs also possess upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) properties, which enable the absorption of longer-wavelength light and conversion into shorter-wavelength light, effectively enhancing the utilization of sunlight [24]. Haiqing Xu et al. constructed a novel broad-spectrum-responsive photocatalyst ZnIn2S4/CQD with a broader solar spectral response and higher photostability than pure ZnIn2S4 [25].

In summary, CQDs-modified ternary MoIn2S4 with a homogeneous structure, similar to the binary cubic phase In2S3, is synthesized by a simple one-pot hydrothermal method. The synthesis process of CQDs-modified MoIn2S4 is greatly simplified by utilizing anhydrous citric acid as both a surfactant and a carbon source. Significantly, the study marks the inaugural application of ternary cubic-phase MoIn2S4 in the field of photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Furthermore, this paper delves into the issue of photocatalytic degradation capability of MoIn2S4/CQDs, specifically in the degradation of methylene blue (MB), and proposes a plausible mechanism for photocatalytic degradation process.

Sodium molybdate dihydrate (Na2MoO4·2H2O), indium(III) chloride tetrahydrate (InCl3·4H2O), anhydrous citric acid (C6H8O7), glucose (C6H12O6), thioacetamide (C2H5NS), methylene blue (C16H18ClN3S), rhodamine B (C28H31ClN2O3), congo red (C32H22N6Na2O6S2), methyl orange (C14H14N3NaO3S), tetracycline (C22H24N2O8), levofloxacin (C18H20FN3O4), anhydrous ethanol (CH3CH2OH), p-benzoquinone (PBQ), tert-butanol (TBA), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were provided by Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (China). All reagents purchased were of analytical grade and were used without further purification, supplemented with deionized water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MoIn2S4 and MoIn2S4/CQDs complexes
ce:section.ce:para: Usually, different amounts of anhydrous citric acid (0 g, 0.5 g, 1 g, 1.5 g, 2 g), sodium molybdate dihydrate (1 mmol), and indium trichloride tetrahydrate (2 mmol) (the molar ratio of Mo and In is 1:2 [8]) were poured into 50 mL of deionized water sequentially, and stirred uniformly, and then thioacetamide (6 mmol) was added and stirred for 30 min until it was completely dissolved. The above suspension was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then held at 160 °C for 12 h. After naturally cooling to room temperature, the sample underwent a washing process involving three rinses with deionized water and ethanol, respectively, followed by centrifugation for collection. Subsequently, it was dried at 70 ℃ for 12 h and finally ground to yield the desired brown product. Finally, the products were labeled according to the varying amounts of citric acid used during the preparation process: MoIn2S4, MoIS/CQDs-0.5 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.5 g, and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g, corresponding to the usage of 0 g, 0.5 g, 1.0 g, 1.5 g, and 2.0 g of citric acid, respectively.
In addition, attempts were made to fabricate MoIn2S4/CQDs photocatalysts by combining MoIn2S4 with other forms of carbon quantum dots. (Detailed preparation methods in the supporting information.).

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD, Bruker D8 Advance) was used to determine the crystal structures of the samples. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Thermo Scientific Nicolette, USA) was employed to meticulously analyze the infrared spectra of the samples. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo ESCALAB 250XI) was employed to analyze the elemental composition of the samples. Field emission scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Model SU8020) was used to examine the morphological characteristics of the samples. Energy-dispersive spectrometry (EDS) and elemental mapping were conducted concurrently with the SEM to provide further information about elemental distribution. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30) provided a detailed examination of the samples' microstructure. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method (BET, Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA) was used to precisely determine the specific surface area and pore volume of the nanomaterials. Photoluminescent spectra (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000, UK) were measured to assess the photoluminescent properties of the samples. Diffuse reflectance spectra were analyzed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer (UV–vis, Hitachi U-3010) equipped with an integrating sphere accessory. Dynamic light scattering (DLS, Malvern Zetasizer Pro, UK) was employed to meticulously analyze the zeta potential of the samples, revealing their surface charge characteristics. Electrochemical impedance spectra (EIS) and Mott-Schottky plots were acquired using an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E) to investigate the electrochemical properties of the materials. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, A300-10/12, Bruker, Germany) was used to analyze the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) during degradation processes involving the nanomaterials.

The photocatalytic degradation performances of MoIn2S4, MoIS/CQDs-0.5 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.5 g, and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g towards a variety of organic pollutants were tested. Taking MB as an example, 10 mg of the photocatalyst was poured into a beaker containing 50 ml of MB solution (40 mg/L, PH = 5.0) prepared with ultrapure water. The solution was then stirred at 500 rpm in a dark environment for 30 min to allow the photocatalyst to reach adsorption/desorption equilibrium with methylene blue. Subsequently, the solution was irradiated with a 300 W Xenon lamp light source (PLS-SXE 300 +/UV) simulated with an AM 1.5 G filter (200 mW·cm−2), positioned approximately 12 cm above the solution. During irradiation, stirring of the solution at 500 rpm was maintained, and the temperature of the solution was kept constant at 25 ℃ through a circulating water system. The natural pH of the solution was preserved throughout the experiment. during the irradiation process, 4 mL aliquots of the degradation solution were withdrawn from the reaction mixture every 10 min. These samples were then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 3 min to separate the solid particles and retain the supernatant. The absorbance of the MB degradation products in the supernatant was measured using a Hitachi U-3010 UV–visible spectrophotometer, allowing us to assess the photocatalytic degradation efficiency. The photocatalytic degradation rate and first-order degradation kinetics were calculated as follows:
(1)

where C0 represents the original absorbance of methylene blue, Ct represents the absorbance of the solution after different times of photocatalytic degradation, C1 represents the absorbance after dark adsorption, K (min−1) denotes the reaction rate constant, and t (min) represents the elapsed time.
The information regarding the Limit of Detection (LOD) and Limit of Quantitation (LOQ) of the calibration curve in the Supporting Information.

Fig. 1 (a) displays the XRD patterns of MoIn2S4, MoIS/CQDs-0.5 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, MoIS/CQDs-1.5 g and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g. All five samples exhibit identical characteristic peaks at 2θ = 23.7°, 27.5°, 33.4°, 43.8°, and 47.9°, which correspond to the (220), (311), (400), (511), and (440) crystallographic facets of the cubic-phase β-In2S3 (PDF#32–0456), while the diffraction peaks at 14.4° and 66.5° correspond to the (002) and (114) crystal planes of cubic 2H-MoS2 (PDF#37–1492). The consistent growth patterns of the XRD peaks in the five samples indicate the synthesis of a substance with a fixed atomic ratio. Besides, the XRD peak heights exhibit no significant intensity variations, and the peak shapes are sharp without any discernible impurity peaks, indicating excellent crystallinity and high purity of the sample. The above results confirm that the successful synthesis of ternary cubic phase MoIn2S4 with a consistent binary cubic In2S3 crystal structure is achieved through Mo atoms doping. The similar fabrication of the ternary transition metal sulfide MoIn2S4 was also reported by Gentian Yue et al. by comparison with standard PDF cards of 2H-MoS2 and β-In2S3, respectively [8]. Moreover, the increasing intensity of the (220) crystal plane peak may be attributed to the influence of loaded CQDs on the growth of MoIn2S4. To further ascertain the successful loading of CQDs, the samples were characterized by FT-IR spectroscopy. In Fig. 1(b), for MoIn2S4, the peak near 3405 cm−1 and 1400 cm−1 can be attributed to the hydroxyl groups (·OH) of surface-absorbed water molecules [25]. For MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface is indicated by the noticeable enhancement of peaks related to the O–H stretching vibration and stretching vibration absorption bands of C = O, and C-O at approximately 1600 cm−1, and 1127 cm−1, respectively [26–28]. The presence of all distinctive peaks associated with CQDs in the FT-IR spectra confirms that the CQDs have been successful loaded on MoIn2S4 [29–31].

Using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), the surface elemental composition and chemical states of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g were further analyzed. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), in the Mo 3d region, the peak at 226.68 eV in yellow is attributed to the 2S orbital of S. The double peaks for Mo 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 at 235.88 eV and 232.78 eV in the pink region and 232.38 eV and 229.28 eV in the green region correspond to the Mo6+ and Mo4+ states, respectively. The peak area of Mo6+ significantly decreases, while the peak area of Mo4+ noticeably increases after loading CQDs, indicating a more substantial presence of tetravalent Mo after modification. This suggests a higher purity of MoIn2S4 because the presence of Mo4+ is attributed to the reduction of Mo6+ (Na2MoO4) to Mo4+ (MoIn2S4) [32]. The presence of In3+ is demonstrated by the In 3d spectrum of MoIn2S4 in Fig. 2(b), where characteristic peaks at binding energies of 452.78 eV and 445.24 eV are assigned to In 3d3/2 and 3d5/2, respectively [33]. Similarly, in Fig. 2(c), both sets of double peaks with binding energies 164.51 eV, 163.41 eV and 162.85 eV, 161.70 eV, corresponded to S 2p1/2 and S 2p3/2 for the S2- anion [34]. The successful synthesis of the MoIn2S4 sample is evidenced by the coexistence of Mo4+, In3+, and S2-. With the loading of CQDs, the peak positions of Mo 3d, In 3d, and S 2p of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g were slightly shifted, which could be attributed to the electronic coupling between MoIn2S4 and CQDs. Moreover, in the C 1 s spectrum of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g shown in Fig. 2(d), binding energies of 289.1 eV, 285.5 eV, and 284.9 eV correspond to C = O, C-O, and C–C bonds, respectively [35]. The presence of CQDs grown in-situ on MoIn2S4 can be confirmed by using XRD, FT-IR spectra, and XPS techniques together [36].

The morphology of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g was determined using Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) images. As depicted in Fig. 3 (a), MoIn2S4 exhibits a nano-flower structure formed by the aggregation of irregular two-dimensional nanosheets, with an approximate diameter ranging from 300 to 400 nm. Conversely, Fig. 3(b) illustrates that MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g consists of a random assembly of smaller nanoparticles, with diameters spanning roughly between 100 to 200 nm. Compare to MoIn2S4, the rougher surface of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g is attributed to the effect of CQDs loading on the MoIn2S4 nanosheets [23]. The larger specific surface area generated by the rough MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g surface provides more reaction sites for catalytic reactions. CQDs were successfully prepared in-situ on MoIn2S4, as evidenced by the uniform distribution of Mo, In, S, and C elements on the surface of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g observed in SEM-mapping (Fig. 3(c-g)) [37]. Fig. 3(h, i) represents high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. In MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, two distinct lattice fringes with spacings of 0.62 nm (Figure I) and 0.32 nm (Figure II) correspond to the (002) crystal planes of MoS2 and CQDs, respectively [38,39]. Fig. S2(a) presents the TEM image of CQDs, revealing their regular spherical morphology with an average size of 4.71 nm (Fig. S2(b)) and uniformly dispersed distribution [40]. Additionally, Fig. S2(c) demonstrates the excellent crystallinity of CQDs, with a lattice fringe width of 0.32 nm, which corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of CQDs. Moreover, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibits a higher abundance of few-layered sheet-like structures after CQDs loading, as evident from the comparative HRTEM images of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. The increase in few-layered sheet structures results in a higher number of active sites on the sample surface, consequently enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the sample [41].

In Fig. 4 (a), both MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibit mesoporous layered structures, as evidenced by the IV-type isotherms and H3-type hysteresis loops observed in Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) measurements [42]. The specific surface area of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g after CQD loading (71.7 m2/g) is approximately 1.9 times higher than that of MoIn2S4 (38.1 m2/g). As shown in Fig. 4(b), the total pore volume of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g after CQD loading (0.13 cm3/g) is approximately 2.2 times that of MoIn2S4 (0.06 cm3/g). The increase in specific surface area and pore volume of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g enhances adsorption capacity for pollutants and improves the sample's adsorption performance [43]. Additionally, the introduction of extra reactive sites for the photocatalytic process mitigates the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, collectively bolstering the overall degradation performance of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g.

Fig. 5 (a) presents the photoluminescence (PL) spectrum of CQDs, revealing a wide emission spectrum in the visible light range. Notably, the emission intensity peaks when the excitation wavelength is set at 400 nm, suggesting that 400 nm serves as the optimal excitation wavelength for CQDs. This can be attributed to the presence of CQDs with different sizes and the existence of carboxyl functional groups on the surface of CQDs [44–46]. The photoluminescent characteristics of CQDs are evidenced by the emission of blue light from CQDs under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (inset in Fig. 5(a)) [47]. Moreover, upconversion photoluminescence (UCPL) effects of CQDs was demonstrated in Fig. 5(b). When the long excitation wavelength ranges from 650 nm to 900 nm, the emission peak of CQDs consistently falls within the visible light range of 450–550 nm [24,25]. The UCPL phenomenon of CQDs improve capability to absorb near-infrared light and convert it into visible light, which enhances the utilization of solar radiation. During the photocatalytic degradation process, more electrons and holes are involved, thereby increasing the utilization of solar energy boost more electron-hole pairs participation in degradation process [48]. As depicted in Fig. 5(c) of UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (DRS), even though MoIn2S4 exhibits an excellent spectral response in the UV and visible, as well as near-infrared spectral regions, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g demonstrates enhanced light absorption ability owing to the redshift of the absorption edge and increased absorption intensity. The above phenomenon is attributed to the UCPL effect of CQDs, which can absorb light in the near-infrared band and convert it into visible light (Fig. 5(b)). Nevertheless, the absorption threshold of the photocatalyst shows a slight blue shift and the photocatalytic performance gradually decreases as the excessive loading of CQDs (MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g). As depicted in Fig. 5(d), the bandgaps of both MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g were determined to be about 1.38 eV using the Kubelka-Munk function [49].

Based on the VB-XPS spectrum (Fig. 6 (a, b)) and the band gap of the samples, the valence band maximum (VBM) potentials for MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g were determined to be 0.52 eV and 0.22 eV, respectively, while the conduction band minimum (CBM) potentials were −0.86 eV and −1.16 eV, respectively [50]. The more negative conduction band of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g allows free electrons to have a stronger reduction ability to convert O2 into ·O2 –. According to the plotting results of the Mott-Schottky curves of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g (Fig. 6(c, d)), the positive slope of the curve indicates that MoIn2S4 is an n-type semiconductor [51]. The semiconductor type of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g remains unchanged and still exhibits n-type semiconductor characteristics after loading CQDs. Only the Flat Band Potential of the sample changes from −0.62 eV of MoIn2S4 to −0.93 eV of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g after loaded with CQDs [52]. The determination of the semiconductor type and band structure of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g provides the basis for deriving the photocatalytic mechanism of the MoIn2S4/CQDs composite system.

In Fig. 7 (a), the transient photocurrent of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g is approximately 8 times that of MoIn2S4, and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g is around 4 times that of MoIn2S4. The increase in photocurrent intensity indicates that the improvement in photogenerated charge carrier separation and migration capability of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g is attributed to the loading of CQDs. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to further compare the performance difference of MoIn2S4 before and after loading CQDs. As shown in Fig. 7(b), the impedance curves of MoIn2S4, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g all conform to the Nyquist pattern, the arc radius of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g is significantly smaller than that of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g. The smaller electrochemical impedance implies that MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g possesses an efficient separation of photogenerated charge carriers and enhances charge transfer capability. This is consistent with the results obtained from transient photocurrent measurements. However, the transient photocurrent intensity of MoIS/CQDs-2.0 g decreases, and the electrochemical impedance increases when the loading amount of CQDs continues to increase. This indicates that the introduction of an appropriate amount of CQDs is essential to enhance the separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated charge carriers in MoIn2S4/CQDs, which results in a higher photocatalytic activity of the sample. In Fig. 7(c), the steady-state PL spectra exhibit the observed PL emission peaks for MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, with the maximum PL emission at 440 nm obtained using an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. From the graph, it can be observed that MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibits a lower electron-hole recombination rate due to its lower PL intensity [53]. Fig. 7(d) illustrates the zeta potentials of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g dispersed in deionized water, which are −27.83 mV and –33.05 mV, respectively. The more negative zeta potential of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g indicates a higher concentration of hydroxyl groups (–OH) on its surface, consistent with the FT-IR results [52]. Given that MB is a positively charged dye, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g with a more negative zeta potential will adsorb more dye molecules, which contributes to the enhancement of photocatalytic performance [54].

In order to investigate the photocatalytic performance of a series of samples, the degradation performance of MoIn2S4 (10 mg) and MoIn2S4/CQDs (10 mg) for MB (40 mg/L) under Xenon lamp irradiation simulating sunlight was compared (200 mW.cm −2), as show in Fig. 8 (a). After 30 min of dark adsorption, the MB removal of MoIn2S4 reached 32.0 %, while the MB removal of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g loaded with CQDs reached 52.7 %. After 60 min of light exposure, the removal of MB increased to 94.4 % for MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g，which is much higher than the 75.0 % of MoIn2S4. In order to further investigate the photocatalytic performance of the samples, degradation kinetic curves were plotted as shown in the inset of Fig. 8(a). According to the first-order degradation kinetic formula, the degradation rate constants for the MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-X (X = 0.5 g, 1.0 g, 1.5 g, 2.0 g) photocatalysts were determined as −0.0166, −0.0283, −0.0355, −0.0251, and −0.0181 min−1, respectively [55]. Among them, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibited the fastest degradation rate. The enhanced adsorption capacity of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g with CQDs loading is primarily attributed to the π-π stacking formed between the aromatic rings of CQDs and organic pollutants containing aromatic rings [56–58]. The increasing degradation rate may be attributed to the more exposed layer-structure after modification, which increases the specific surface area and pore size of the sample to further increase the number of active sites [59]. It is in accordance with the HRTEM images. Moreover, as electron transfer centers, CQD exhibits strong ability to suppress electron-hole pair recombination [60]. To investigate the impact of photocatalyst dosage on degradation performance, a comparison was conducted in Fig. S3(a) between the degradation of 40 mg/L MB in the absence of a photocatalyst and that with the addition of 5 mg, 10 mg, and 15 mg of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. The observation revealed that pure MB exhibits photostability, degrading by only 8 % after 60 min of illumination. Fig. S3(b) represents the degradation kinetic curves, 15 mg of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibited the highest degradation kinetic constant of −0.0832 min−1. Therefore, the degradation rate of MB is closely related to the dosage of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. Notably, a comparative analysis was also conducted to evaluate the degradation performance of MB using MoIn2S4/CQDs composites modified with various forms of carbon quantum dots, as depicted in Fig. S3(c). However, none of the degradation performances were superior to that of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. In addition, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g also exhibited potential degradation performance towards other organic compounds (Fig. S3(d)). After 60 min of illumination, the degradation performance of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g (10 mg) reached 70.7 % for rhodamine B (RhB), 69.4 % for methyl orange (MO), and 97.3 % for congo red (CR). For pharmaceuticals, it achieved 86.0 % degradation for tetracycline (TC) and 78.1 % for levofloxacin (LEV). MoIn2S4/CQDs photocatalytic efficiency compared to the photocatalytic degradation performance reported for other ternary indium-based sulfide photocatalysts in the past three years (Table S1), as well as for other composites containing CQDs photocatalysts during the same period (Table S2), show its high performance.

To verify the stability of the sample, 5 cycles of photocatalytic degradation tests were conducted using 10 mg of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, maintaining the same concentration as in the initial photocatalytic degradation tests (Methodology for photocatalytic cycle experiments in the Supporting Information). Fig. 8(b) shows that MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g still has a degradation efficiency of over 90 % after 5 cycles. This indicates that the sample has structural stability and good durability. To explore the contribution of the main active substances in photocatalysis, as depicted in Fig. 8(c), benzoquinone (PBQ), tert-butanol (TBA), and disodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid (EDTA-2Na) were added as capture agents for ·O2 –, ·OH, and h+, respectively, to sequester the reactive oxygen species (ROS) generated during the degradation process. The degradation efficiency of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g decreased from the initial 94.3 % to 56.4 % (PBQ), 76.4 % (TBA), and 65.6 % (EDTA-2Na), respectively, after the addition of the trapping agents. Therefore, considering the inhibitory effects of the three types of sacrificial agents on the degradation of MB by MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, ·O2 – is likely the most prominent reactive species involved in the degradation process of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. The capture experiment results are consistent with the band structure diagrams of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g (Fig. 8(d)). The conduction band (CB) potentials of MoIn2S4 (−0.86 eV) and MoIS/CQDs-2 (−1.16 eV) are negative than the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 – (−0.33 eV vs Normal Hydrogen Electrode (NHE)) [61]. This indicates that both MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g can reduce O2 to ·O2 –. Whereas, the inability of OH– to be oxidized to ·OH is attributed to the valence band positions of MoIn2S4 (0.52 eV) and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g (0.22 eV), both of which are more positive than the standard oxidation potential of ·OH/OH– (1.99 eV vs NHE) [62]. Therefore, ·O2 – is identified as the primary reactive species during the degradation process of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g.

To better elucidate the photocatalytic reaction mechanism of MB degradation for the samples before and after modification, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was conducted on the active species of MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g after 0 min and 10 min of irradiation with light. As shown in Fig. 9 (a, b), ·O2 – and ·OH were not detected in both MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g at 0 min of light exposure, while the ·O2 – and ·OH EPR signal intensities increased after 10 min of light exposure, which demonstrates that both MoIn2S4 and MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g have catalytic properties [63]. In Fig. 9(c, d), the relative intensities of the EPR signals for ·O2 – and ·OH in MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g were observed to be stronger than those in MoIn2S4 after a 10 min light exposure. It is suggested that the introduction of CQDs into the MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g system can enhance the production of reactive species under light conditions [64]. Comparing the changes in the signals of •O2 – and •OH, it is evident that the amplification of •O2 – is more pronounced, which signifies that •O2 – is the predominant reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation process of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g [51]. The results of EPR spectroscopy align with the conclusions drawn from the capture experiments (Fig. 8(c)).

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the photocatalytic enhancement mechanism of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g under simulated solar irradiation was proposed. A schematic representation of the photocatalytic mechanism of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g can be found in Fig. 10 . The increased presence of few-layer structures in MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g leads to an expanded contact area between the sample and the pollutant (MB). Consequently, it enhances the adsorption capacity of the sample, thereby improving the ultimate degradation efficiency. When MoIn2S4 is irradiated by light, photogenerated electrons and holes are generated in the valence band (VB). The excited electrons transition to the conduction band (CB) and subsequently transfer to CQDs (Eq. 3). Since the CB potential of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g (−1.16 eV) is negative to the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 – (−0.33 eV), the electrons on the CQDs react with the O2 molecules on the surface of the catalyst to produce a large amount of ·O2 – (Eq. 4). Reactive oxygen species (ROS) can react with MB to decompose it into organic small molecules and other degradation products (Eq. 5) [65].

Compared with MoIn2S4, the superior photocatalytic performance of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g can be attributed to three key factors: Firstly, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g has a large specific surface area and abundant active sites. In the synthesis process of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, anhydrous citric acid as a surfactant can inhibit the aggregation of MoIn2S4, exposing a large number of layered structures, which is conducive to the improvement of specific surface area and the uniform dispersion of highly catalytic active CQDs on the photocatalytic surface. Therefore, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g benefits from an increase in specific surface area and active sites, significantly improving photocatalytic degradation efficiency. Secondly, the UCPL effect exhibited by CQDs significantly broadens the solar absorption range of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g. Due to the UCPL effect of CQD, which can convert near-infrared wavelengths to shorter wavelengths, the light absorption ability of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g is improved and the absorption edge undergoes a red shift. This is beneficial for improving the solar light utilization efficiency and broadening the light response range of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, allowing more photons to be absorbed to produce a large number of photo generated charge carriers. Finally, CQDs as an effective electron acceptor and transfer center, can significantly improve the separation efficiency of photo generated carriers. Under illumination, photo generated electrons in MoIn2S4 can quickly transfer to CQDs with high electron mobility, significantly improving the migration efficiency of photo generated carriers and effectively suppressing photo generated carrier recombination. Therefore, compared with MoIn2S4, MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibits lower photoluminescence intensity, higher photocurrent density, and reduced electrochemical impedance.

Therefore, the few-layer structure of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g provides more active sites for the reaction, the UCPL effect of CQDs enhances the light-absorbing ability of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, and the newly established electron transport channel between MoIn2S4 and CQDs accelerates the electron-directed transfer. The synergistic effect of the above three points effectively suppresses the recombination of photo generated carriers and promotes charge migration, enabling MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g to have efficient degradation ability for MB. This work proposes potential mechanisms by which CQDs enhance the photocatalytic performance of MoIn2S4, which provides a basis for the subsequent study of the mutual enhancement mode of action between MoIn2S4 and carbon-based materials.

In summary, we have prepared a structurally stable MoIn2S4/CQDs photocatalyst with a few layers using a simple one-pot hydrothermal method by employing anhydrous citric acid as a surfactant and carbon source. MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g exhibited the optimal photocatalytic performance, achieving a 94.4 % degradation rate of MB after 60 min of irradiation with a 300 W Xenon lamp, simulating sunlight. The investigation revealed that the addition of anhydrous citric acid surfactant results in MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g possessing a more few-layer and porous structure, conducive to the adsorption of pollutants (MB). The increased specific surface area provides more active sites for the reaction, consequently reducing the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes. The upconversion fluorescence properties of CQDs enhance the solar utilization efficiency of MoIS/CQDs-1.0 g, while the increased light absorption capability promotes the generation of more photoinduced charge carriers. Simultaneously, the introduction of CQDs also facilitates charge transfer, thereby mitigating the losses associated with the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes in the photocatalytic process. These three synergistic effects collectively enhance the photocatalytic activity of MoIn2S4/CQDs. This research lays a foundation for comprehending the properties of novel photocatalysts like MoIn2S4. Moreover, it provides valuable insights for designing more efficient photocatalysts involving MoIn2S4/carbon-based nanomaterials.
